summary: George Hotz reflects on five years of building tinygrad, a minimal deep learning framework that now stands at 18,935 lines of code with a team of 6 people. He argues that the path to competing with NVIDIA is through software sovereignty, not hardware—pointing out that AMD, Amazon, Tesla, and Groq have all made good chips but failed at training because they lack the software stack. Tinygrad is working toward zero dependencies and LLVM removal to drive AMD GPUs with pure Python. Hotz critiques mainstream software development as 98% workarounds for other code, advocating for Elon's principle that 'the best part is no part.' The tiny corp operates as a 'deconstructed company' with public Discord, GitHub, AMD contracts negotiated on Twitter, and a mission to commoditize the petaflop.
key_points: Software sovereignty, not chip design, is the hard part—Google and NVIDIA dominate training because of software, not silicon
Tinygrad aims for zero dependencies (pure Python only) and will be about 20,000 lines when complete
98% of software lines are workarounds for issues in other parts of the codebase, a dysfunction that also plagues organizations
The tiny corp operates as a 'deconstructed company' with radical transparency—public Discord, GitHub, and contracts negotiated on Twitter
The company funds itself through $2M/year in computer sales and an AMD contract for MI350X MLPerf benchmarks
money_quotes: Only a fool begins by taping out a chip; it's expensive and not the hard part.
Once you have a fully sovereign software stack capable of training SOTA models, the chip is so easy.
I think this is so bad that 98% of lines of software are basically this in some way shape or form.
Make the requirements less dumb. The best part is no part.
Most requirements in software exist to maintain compatibility with other abstractions.
Each piece of code in that stack isn't focused on the goal. It's focused on the other pieces of code.
Like how fancy chefs will deconstruct dishes, the tiny corp is a deconstructed company.
Our mission is to commoditize the petaflop.
key_insight: The path to competing with NVIDIA isn't building better chips—it's building a fully sovereign, minimal software stack, because software is the actual moat.
